retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The use of cocaine as an anesthetic agent will require careful consideration by the clinician, who should involve the pharmacist in dosing and medication reconciliation. If nursing is administering the drug, they should also coordinate with the clinician and the pharmacist to ensure proper administration and patient safety. It can also be important to know whether the patient has ever had an issue with misusing cocaine as a recreational drug, which may prompt the use of a different agent for the procedure.

retrieved_docs.content: When using cocaine as a therapeutic agent or when misused by a patient requires full interprofessional team collaboration, open communication channels, and coordination of actions and interventions for safe and effective treatment with minimal adverse effects. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine hydrochloride topical solution contains 4% cocaine. It is not FDA-approved but allowed for only the following indications: topical anesthesia of the mucous membranes of the oral, laryngeal, and nasal cavities. Its vasoconstrictive properties also make it useful for the temporary treatment of epistaxis before cauterization or packing, which is considered off-label use. This activity reviews the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, toxicity, dosing, pharmacodynamics, and monitoring of cocaine pertinent for interprofessional team members when used as indicated in the clinical setting.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural (Archived) -- Technique or Treatment -- Patient-controlled Epidural Analgesia (PCEA)
retrieved_docs.content: This technique was described in 1988 by Gambling for the first time. [18] The implementation of this method allows the patient to adjust the time of local anesthetic dosing to contractions and modify the intervals as labor progresses. One of the downsides of this method is the elevated cost of the equipment when compared to the CEP technique. Also, more training and staff education are involved. On the other hand, PCEA has shown lower total dose usage of bupivacaine and ropivacaine compared to CEI. [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Suicide: Assessment and Management -- History and Physical -- Clinical Vignette 2
retrieved_docs.content: A 36-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance at 3 am with thoughts of suicide after using cocaine. His medical record indicates a longstanding history of cocaine use disorder with a pattern of becoming suicidal after use. Previously, when the patient was admitted for inpatient psychiatric treatment, he demanded discharge after a day. You gently wake him up and try to interview him, but he raises his fist and screams and curses that you are in his space and need to get out.

retrieved_docs.title: Cocaine Toxicity -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Over the past three decades, the rates of overdose from cocaine have gradually increased. In the United States, deaths involving cocaine range from 0.9-1.6 per 100,000 population. Only in the last five years have the rates started to decrease to 0.78 per 100,000 population. People who inject cocaine into the neck veins have been known to develop pneumothorax, thrombophlebitis, hemothorax, and myositis. In addition, there are reports that intravenous injections can be associated with aneurysms of vessels, resulting in rupture, obstruction, and fistula formation. When cocaine is combined with other illicit and prescription drugs, the mortality rates are also high. More important, cocaine and alcohol have been associated with a 16 fold increase in the risk of suicide than either agent alone. Individuals who use cocaine often select a violent method for self-harm. Finally, cocaine use during pregnancy is also associated with adverse perinatal outcomes. [14] [15] (Level V)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pain (ألم بطني) is clinically associated with cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين), indicating that patients who consume cocaine may present with abdominal discomfort as a manifestation of the drug’s systemic effects. Recognizing this relationship is important for clinicians when evaluating unexplained abdominal pain, as it prompts consideration of substance use history and guides appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1690
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine withdrawal is recognized as a diagnostic criterion within the broader category of substance‑related disorders, meaning that the presence of withdrawal symptoms after cessation of cocaine use helps clinicians identify and classify substance‑related disorders. This relationship is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1855
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), abdominal pain (ألم بطني), and increased blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم). The vasoconstrictive and pro‑thrombotic effects of cocaine can precipitate acute myocardial ischemia, manifesting as unstable angina, while its sympathomimetic activity commonly raises systemic arterial pressure. Additionally, cocaine‑induced visceral vasospasm or ischemia may present as abdominal pain. Recognizing these interrelated clinical manifestations is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management of patients who use cocaine.

Collectively, these entities and relationships demonstrate that alcohol, cocaine, heroin, hallucinogens, opioids, and other psychoactive drugs are interlinked through shared diagnostic categories, dependence mechanisms, poisoning risks, and pharmacologic interventions, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment, and prevention of toxic complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with arterial erosion (تسلخ الشرايين), indicating that individuals who consume cocaine are at risk for damage to the arterial walls. This relationship highlights the vascular complications of cocaine consumption and underscores the importance of monitoring for arterial injury in patients with a history of cocaine use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use is clinically associated with both arterial erosion and increased blood pressure. The vascular damage manifested as arterial erosion reflects the harmful effects of cocaine on the arterial wall, while the stimulant properties of cocaine contribute to elevated blood pressure. Recognizing these interrelated complications is important for clinicians when evaluating patients who use cocaine, as they may require monitoring for vascular injury and hypertension and appropriate interventions to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 889
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine withdrawal is recognized as a diagnostic criterion for substance‑related disorders, indicating that the presence of withdrawal symptoms from cocaine contributes to the classification of these disorders. Substance‑related disorders are organized under the broader category of withdrawal and relapse effects, and they include the drug withdrawal syndrome (SMQ) as one of their members. The drug withdrawal syndrome (SMQ) also encompasses specific treatment approaches such as maintenance therapy for opioid substances, reflecting the role of opioid maintenance programs in managing withdrawal. Within the overarching framework of withdrawal and relapse effects, rebound tachycardia is identified as a specific physiological manifestation that can occur during withdrawal or after relapse. This interconnected structure highlights how withdrawal phenomena, diagnostic criteria, syndromic classifications, therapeutic strategies, and acute cardiovascular responses are linked in the clinical understanding and management of substance‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 253
retrieved_docs.content: concepts depict a comprehensive view of substance‑related disorders, encompassing diagnostic criteria (e.g., cocaine withdrawal), various substance‑induced psychiatric and behavioral states, hierarchical classifications across languages, specific syndromes such as drug withdrawal, therapeutic approaches like opioid maintenance, and physiological sequelae such as rebound tachycardia. This integrated framework aids clinicians in recognizing the full spectrum of substance‑related conditions, their diagnostic components, and associated treatment and complication pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pain and cocaine use (تعاطة الكوكائين) are both clinically associated with malignant hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الخبيث). The occurrence of abdominal pain may serve as a clinical clue indicating the presence of malignant hypertension, while the use of cocaine is recognized as a precipitating factor that can trigger or exacerbate malignant hypertension. Recognizing these associations is important for clinicians to promptly identify severe hypertension in patients presenting with abdominal discomfort or a history of cocaine use, enabling timely intervention and improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2243
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine is classified as a member of the analgesics and anesthetics group, reflecting its pharmacologic properties as a potent local anesthetic and pain‑relieving agent. In addition, cocaine is categorized under controlled substances, indicating that its use, distribution, and possession are subject to strict regulatory controls due to its potential for abuse and dependence. This dual classification highlights both its therapeutic applications in medical settings and the need for careful regulation to prevent misuse.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2838
retrieved_docs.content: تعاطي الكوكائين (cocaine use) is clinically associated with زيادة ضغط الدم (elevated blood pressure). This connection indicates that individuals who use cocaine are at increased risk of developing hypertension, and clinicians should be vigilant in monitoring blood pressure in patients with cocaine use to manage potential cardiovascular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2870
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين) is clinically associated with both unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and arterial dissection (تسلخ الشرايين). This interconnection indicates that exposure to cocaine can precipitate acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina as well as serious vascular injuries like arterial dissection. Recognizing cocaine use as a contributing factor is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of patients presenting with these cardiovascular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1456
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine and cannabinoids are both categorized as controlled substances, indicating that they are regulated drug classes subject to legal and medical oversight. Each of these substances is recognized in drug classification systems that document their presence or characteristics, the timing of detection, the type of body fluid in which they may be identified, and their nominal classification. In addition to this regulatory context, cannabinoids have a specific pharmacological relationship with cannabinoid receptors, meaning that they exert their effects by interacting with these receptors in the body. Understanding that cocaine and cannabinoids share a controlled‑substance status while cannabinoids also engage distinct receptor pathways informs both the legal handling of these drugs and the clinical considerations for detection, monitoring, and therapeutic intervention.
